versions.simple_zh:
🙌 肩膀「凍結」？無痛解凍術讓您恢復自由！

「無痛肩關節鬆動術（Silent Manipulation）」是專門治療冰凍肩（肩關節沾黏）的技術。特點是：您在完全清醒、完全不痛的情況下接受治療。

📌 誰需要這個治療？

  • 手舉不高（前舉不到 100°，或手無法碰到背後腰部）
  • 吃藥、打針、做物理治療 3～6 個月後仍沒有明顯改善
  • 疼痛已影響睡眠、無法自己穿衣或梳頭

🔧 治療的三個步驟

  1. 先讓肩膀麻木：醫師在超音波導引下，精準施打頸部神經阻斷，讓肩膀完全不痛。
  2. 消炎注射：在關節處注射少量類固醇，預防術後腫脹疼痛。
  3. 鬆開沾黏：醫師輕巧地活動您的肩膀，您可能聽到「喀」一聲——這是正常的！那代表沾黏已鬆開。

⚠️ 注意事項

  • 治療後手臂會麻約 12 小時，嚴禁自行開車，請安排家人接送或計程車。
  • 麻醉消退後即開始主動活動，隔天移除三角巾。
  • 術後 8 週是防止再沾黏的黃金期，請積極配合復健運動。

📊 治療效果

測量項目治療前治療後3個月
手向前舉高約 99°約 156°
手向側舉高約 76°約 153°
手向外旋轉約 3°約 46°

✅ 追蹤 1,665 例——無一例骨折或脫位；僅約 7% 的人需再做一次。
versions.professional_zh:
解鎖冰封的肩膀：無痛肩關節鬆動術 (Silent Manipulation)

「Silent Manipulation」是一種強調優雅且精準的技術。與傳統需全身麻醉的激烈治療不同，此技術源自日本，著重於細膩的手法與受控的力道，能在您完全清醒且無痛的狀態下，安靜且有節奏地解開關節沾黏。

一、適合的對象（適應症）

  • 活動極度受限：手向前舉不超過 100°、手無法向外旋轉，或「手摸背」高度低於腰部以下。
  • 保守治療無效：經過 3 到 6 個月的藥物、打針或物理治療，進步仍不明顯。
  • 嚴重影響生活：疼痛導致失眠，或因肩膀僵硬而無法穿衣、梳頭與工作。

二、療程三階段與注意事項

1. 治療前：準備與禁忌

  • 著裝方便露出肩膀的衣物。
  • 嚴禁自駕：治療後手臂會暫時麻痺 12 小時，請安排接送。
  • 禁忌症：急性骨折、關節感染、嚴重骨質疏鬆或單側肺部手術史。

2. 治療中（三步驟）

  1. 超音波導引神經阻斷：精準阻斷頸部神經傳導（斜角肌間阻斷），使肩膀無痛。
  2. 肩關節類固醇注射：注射至前肩關節、喙肱韌帶與三角肌下滑囊，減少發炎。
  3. 鬆動術（精準鬆動）：系統化鬆開沾黏關節囊；可能聽到「喀啦」聲，代表關節囊成功鬆解。

3. 治療後：黃金康復期

  • 三角巾固定至隔天麻醉消退後移除，隨即開始主動使用。
  • 術後 8 週內為防止再沾黏黃金期，需積極復健。

三、臨床數據（n = 1,665）

關節活動度治療前（平均）治療後3個月（平均）
手向前抬高（前屈）98.8°155.5°
手向側抬高（外展）75.6°152.9°
手向外旋轉（外旋）2.7°45.9°

安全性：無任何骨折或脫位等嚴重併發症。復發率：約 7%。
versions.english:
Painless Shoulder Manipulation (Silent Manipulation)

Silent Manipulation is a technique for treating frozen shoulder (adhesive capsulitis) while the patient remains fully awake and pain-free. Unlike manipulation under general anesthesia (MUA), this approach uses a regional nerve block to achieve analgesia during the procedure.

Indications

  • Forward flexion < 100°, restricted external rotation, or limited internal rotation (hand-behind-back below waist)
  • Failed conservative management (medications, injections, physiotherapy) for ≥ 3–6 months
  • Significant functional impairment: sleep disturbance, inability to dress or groom

Contraindications

  • Acute fracture, joint infection, severe osteoporosis, history of ipsilateral lung surgery

Procedure (3 steps)

  1. Ultrasound-guided interscalene nerve block — eliminates shoulder pain; arm numbness lasts ~12 hours
  2. Intraarticular corticosteroid injection (glenohumeral joint, coracohumeral ligament, subacromial bursa) — reduces post-procedure inflammation
  3. Systematic joint mobilization — gentle, rhythmic capsular release; audible or palpable "pop" is expected and indicates successful release

Post-procedure Instructions

  • Arm sling for 12 hours; do not drive (arm numbness lasts ~12 hours)
  • Begin active range-of-motion exercises once numbness resolves (usually next day)
  • 8-week rehabilitation window — critical to prevent re-adhesion and restore muscle strength

Outcomes (n = 1,665)

ROM MeasurementPre-treatment (mean)3 months post (mean)
Forward flexion98.8°155.5°
Abduction75.6°152.9°
External rotation2.7°45.9°

Safety: zero fractures or dislocations in 1,665 tracked cases. Recurrence rate ~7%.

versions.simple_zh:
🦵 膝蓋扭傷後的內側和後小腿疼痛

膝蓋扭傷後，若同時出現膝蓋內側疼痛和小腿後側疼痛，可能涉及多個部位受傷，以下是最常見的幾種原因：

🔍 可能的受傷部位

  • 內側小腿肌肉（「網球腿」）：最常見。小腿內側的腓腸肌在肌腱交界處撕裂，疼痛會延伸到膝蓋後內側，在膝蓋伸直合併腳踝往上彎時特別容易發生。
  • 膝蓋內側韌帶（MCL）：膝蓋受外側力量撞擊或向外扭傷時容易受傷，造成內側疼痛與腫脹，有時合併小腿肌肉同時受傷。
  • 膝蓋後方韌帶（PCL）：受到由前往後的撞擊或膝蓋過度彎曲時受傷，可能引起後方不穩定感。有時撕裂的韌帶碎片會卡住，造成內側疼痛。
  • 後內側角結構（POL、半膜肌）：位於膝蓋後內側，常與 MCL 或 PCL 損傷同時發生，在 61.7% 的膝部創傷中可見撕裂。

🩺 醫師如何評估？

  • 理學檢查：按壓膝蓋內側、測試外翻穩定度及 PCL 後方穩定度，並壓診小腿近端肌腱交界處。
  • 超音波：快速評估小腿腓腸肌撕裂、血腫與積液情況。
  • MRI：金標準，可一次看清所有韌帶和肌肉的損傷程度。

💊 初步處理

RICE 原則：Rest 休息、Ice 冰敷、Compression 加壓包紮、Elevation 抬高患肢。根據損傷嚴重程度安排物理治療，嚴重時才需手術治療。
versions.professional_zh:
膝扭傷後的內側膝痛及後小腿痛

膝部扭傷後，若出現後小腿疼痛、內側膝痛及壓痛的組合，最常見的損傷可能性包括：內側腓腸肌（網球腿）、內側副韌帶 (MCL)、後十字韌帶 (PCL)，或後內側角結構（包含後斜韌帶 (POL) 和半膜肌複合體）。

相關解剖學結構

內側膝部由一組相互連接的複雜結構穩定：

  • 淺層 MCL (Superficial MCL)：從股骨內上髁延伸至脛骨近端內側，約在脛骨平台遠端 2 公分處。
  • 深層 MCL (Deep MCL)（半月板股骨和半月板脛骨韌帶）：提供直接的半月板穩定作用。
  • 後斜韌帶 (POL)：後內側角的關鍵組成部分，將半膜肌肌腱連接到內側半月板和關節囊。
  • 半膜肌肌腱 (Semimembranosus tendon)：附著於脛骨後內側，其擴展部分形成斜膕韌帶；有助於動態穩定性，並在屈曲時帶動內側半月板後角。
  • 內側腓腸肌 (Medial gastrocnemius)：起源於股骨後內側髁，構成小腿內側的大部分肌肉；其遠端肌腱交界處的損傷是最常見的「網球腿」原因。

常見受傷機制與原因

  • MCL 損傷：通常由膝關節伸直或略微屈曲時，受到外翻（外展）和外旋力量所致。MCL 是最常受傷的側副韌帶，且常伴隨 ACL 損傷發生。單純 MCL 損傷表現為內側關節線壓痛和疼痛，但後小腿疼痛並非典型特徵，除非同時合併後側結構損傷。
  • PCL 損傷：發生在腳固定時脛骨受到後向力量，或跌倒時膝關節過度屈曲。PCL 損傷的症狀通常比 ACL 撕裂輕微，包括輕微積液和輕度不穩定。值得注意的是，部分 PCL 撕裂（特別是後內側束）可能在屈曲時，因撕裂殘端卡在內側脛股關節中而引起內側關節線壓痛。PCL 扭傷亦是後膝痛的原因之一。
  • 後內側角損傷：涉及 POL、半膜肌肌腱和相關結構。POL 撕裂發生在 61.7% 的膝部創傷案例中，並與 MCL 撕裂、半膜肌肌腱附著點撕裂、內側半月板撕裂和 PCL 撕裂有高度相關性。這些損傷在 94.7% 的案例中屬於多韌帶損傷模式。
  • 內側腓腸肌損傷（網球腿）：是急性後內側小腿疼痛的常見原因。內側腓腸肌在遠端肌腱交界處的損傷最為常見。這可能發生在膝伸直和踝背屈時的突然離心負荷。

臨床相關性與診斷

內側膝部壓痛和後小腿疼痛的組合提示以下可能性：

  • 合併 MCL 和內側腓腸肌損傷：由外翻機制和腓腸肌離心負荷所致。
  • PCL 扭傷合併後內側角損傷：特別是如果病史中有後向受力或過度屈曲。
  • 單純內側腓腸肌拉傷：疼痛轉移（牽涉痛）至內側膝部區域。

體格檢查應評估 MCL 在 30° 和完全伸直下的外翻壓力測試、PCL 的後抽屜和後下垂徵象，並觸診近端小腿內側腓腸肌肌腱交界處。MRI 成像可用於鑑定韌帶損傷和肌肉拉傷，而超音波則適合初步評估腓腸肌損傷。
versions.english:
Medial Knee Pain and Posterior Calf Pain after Knee Sprain

When a patient presents with the combination of posterior calf pain, medial knee pain, and tenderness following a knee sprain, the most common injury possibilities include: medial gastrocnemius ("tennis leg"), medial collateral ligament (MCL), posterior cruciate ligament (PCL), or posteromedial corner structures (including the posterior oblique ligament [POL] and semimembranosus complex).

Relevant Anatomy

The medial knee is stabilized by an interconnected complex of structures:

  • Superficial MCL: Extends from the medial femoral epicondyle to the proximal medial tibia, approximately 2 cm distal to the tibial plateau.
  • Deep MCL (meniscofemoral and meniscotibial ligaments): Provides direct meniscal stabilization.
  • Posterior Oblique Ligament (POL): A key component of the posteromedial corner, connecting the semimembranosus tendon to the medial meniscus and joint capsule.
  • Semimembranosus tendon: Attaches to the posteromedial tibia; its expansions form the oblique popliteal ligament; contributes to dynamic stability and draws the medial meniscal posterior horn during flexion.
  • Medial gastrocnemius: Originates from the posteromedial femoral condyle, comprising most of the medial calf musculature; injuries at the distal myotendinous junction are the most common cause of "tennis leg."

Injury Mechanisms

  • MCL injury: Typically from valgus (abduction) and external rotation forces with the knee in extension or slight flexion. MCL is the most frequently injured collateral ligament, often co-occurring with ACL tears. Isolated MCL injury presents with medial joint-line tenderness and pain; posterior calf pain is not a typical feature unless posterior structures are also involved.
  • PCL injury: Occurs with posterior-directed tibial force (foot planted) or knee hyperflexion during a fall. PCL tears are generally milder than ACL tears (mild effusion, slight instability). Notably, partial PCL tears (especially the posteromedial bundle) can cause medial joint-line tenderness when the torn stump catches in the medial tibiofemoral compartment during flexion. PCL sprains are also a cause of posterior knee pain.
  • Posteromedial corner injury: Involves POL, semimembranosus tendon, and associated structures. POL tears occur in 61.7% of knee trauma cases, with high correlation to MCL tears, semimembranosus avulsions, medial meniscus tears, and PCL tears. These injuries follow a multi-ligament injury pattern in 94.7% of cases.
  • Medial gastrocnemius injury (Tennis leg): A common cause of acute posteromedial calf pain. Injury at the distal myotendinous junction is most frequent, typically from sudden eccentric loading with the knee extended and ankle dorsiflexed.

Clinical Correlation and Diagnosis

The combination of medial knee tenderness and posterior calf pain suggests:

  • Combined MCL and medial gastrocnemius injury: From valgus mechanism plus gastrocnemius eccentric loading.
  • PCL sprain with posteromedial corner injury: Especially if the mechanism involved posterior-directed force or hyperflexion.
  • Isolated medial gastrocnemius strain: Referred pain to the medial knee region.

Physical examination should assess valgus stress testing of the MCL at 30° and full extension, posterior drawer and posterior sag for PCL, and palpation of the proximal medial gastrocnemius myotendinous junction. MRI can identify ligament injuries and muscle strains, while ultrasound is appropriate for initial assessment of gastrocnemius injury.

Source: Openevidence

versions.simple_zh:
🩸 告別慢性疼痛：經動脈微細血管栓塞術 (TAME) 懶人包

長期被關節炎、五十肩、足底筋膜炎等折磨，吃藥復健卻無法斷根？您的疼痛根源可能是受損組織旁長出的「異常新生血管」，這些血管不僅造成發炎，還會帶著敏感神經一起長入，讓疼痛越來越難消。

TAME（微細動脈栓塞術）就是精準「截斷」這些異常血管，從根源解決問題。

📌 誰適合做 TAME？

  • 疼痛超過 3–6 個月，且吃藥、復健、打針都無明顯效果
  • 退化性膝關節炎、五十肩、網球肘、媽媽手、足底筋膜炎、拇趾外翻痛等
  • 慢性下背痛、肩頸痠痛、慢性骨盆腔疼痛

🔧 兩種做法，醫師幫您選最合適的

類型在哪裡做？適合哪些部位？
簡易型 (sTAME)門診，超音波導引手指關節炎、媽媽手、足底筋膜炎、拇趾外翻、腳趾痛
傳統型 (TAME)醫院血管攝影室退化性膝關節炎、五十肩、網球肘/高爾夫球肘、慢性下背痛、骨盆腔痛

⚡ 治療過程中，我會有什麼感覺？

手術只需局部麻醉。打藥的瞬間，您原本痛的地方可能有短暫的「發熱或痠痛感」——這叫「誘發痛」，是正常現象，代表醫師準確打到痛源了！幾分鐘後感覺就會消失。

📊 效果好不好？

  • 退化性膝關節炎：術後 6 個月成功率 86.3%，3 年後仍 79.8%
  • 五十肩：3 個月成功率 84%，1–3 年後 88–94% 完全無痛
  • 足底筋膜炎：術後 1 週內疼痛開始減輕，3 個月恢復正常活動
  • 整體 6 個月–2 年成功率：70–90%

⚠️ 術後注意事項

  • 傳統型 TAME：術後需確實加壓穿刺部位（鼠蹊或手腕），預防瘀血；瘀血通常 1 週內自然消退
  • 術後先休息，避免當天劇烈活動
  • 術後 1 週開始感受止痛效果，1–6 個月內疼痛持續改善
versions.professional_zh:
經動脈微細血管栓塞術 (TAME) — 臨床指引

一、治療機制

慢性疼痛的病理核心為受損組織周邊的異常新生血管 (CIBAS) 與伴隨侵入的感覺神經末梢。TAME 透過注入暫時性栓塞劑（如 IPM/CS）精準封堵目標異常血管，促使異常神經萎縮，從根源阻斷持續性發炎迴路。

二、適應症與禁忌症

適應症：保守治療（藥物、復健、局部注射）無效達 3–6 個月以上的中重度慢性疼痛，包括退化性膝關節炎、沾黏性肩關節囊炎（五十肩）、肱骨外/內上髁炎（網球肘/高爾夫球肘）、髕骨肌腱炎（跳躍膝）、慢性下背痛、斜方肌肌痛、慢性骨盆腔疼痛症候群、手部/拇指關節炎、狄奎凡氏症、足底筋膜炎、拇趾外翻疼痛、幻肢痛。

禁忌症：膝關節第 4 期退化（建議人工關節置換）、嚴重肌腱或半月板斷裂（需手術修復）、局部活動性感染、嚴重動脈硬化、惡性腫瘤。

三、術前評估

建議術前 MRI：① 排除需手術的結構性破壞；② 確認是否有軟骨下骨髓病變 (BMLs)——BMLs 陽性者術後止痛效果尤佳，且術後 MRI 可見病灶縮小修復。

四、治療方式

項目sTAME（簡易型）TAME（傳統型）
設備超音波導引（門診）DSA 數位減影血管攝影室
適應部位手部/指關節炎、媽媽手、足底筋膜炎、拇趾外翻、蹠趾關節痛退化性膝關節炎、五十肩、網球肘/高爾夫球肘、慢性下背痛、骨盆腔痛
優點無 X 光輻射、門診執行、穿刺孔極小可精準定位深層/大關節異常血管
風險極低Heparin 過敏風險（極低）、局部血腫/假性動脈瘤（需確實壓迫）

五、術中重點：誘發痛 (Provocation Pain)

術中注射栓塞劑時，靶血管所在部位出現短暫「發熱/痠痛」（誘發痛），為成功定位目標異常血管的重要指標，幾分鐘內自行消退。

六、臨床成效摘要

疾病6 個月成功率長期維持
退化性膝關節炎86.3%3 年後 79.8%
沾黏性肩關節囊炎（五十肩）84%（3 個月）1–3 年 88–94% 無痛
網球肘（肱骨外上髁炎）88%2 年 MRI 顯示肌腱修復
慢性下背痛79%（3 個月）24 個月 57%
拇指基部關節炎100%24 個月穩定維持

七、術後照護

  • 傳統型 TAME：確實加壓穿刺部位（鼠蹊/手腕動脈），預防血腫；微瘀血通常 1 週內自然消退
  • 止痛效果分兩階段：術後 1 週內快速緩解（神經阻斷期）→ 1–6 個月持續下降（組織修復期）
  • 整體 6 個月–2 年臨床成功率 70–90%
versions.english:
Transcatheter Arterial Microembolization (TAME) — Clinical Overview

Mechanism of Action

TAME targets chronic inflammation-based abnormal sprouting vessels (CIBAS) — pathological neovascularization found in chronically painful tissue. By injecting a temporary embolic agent (typically imipenem/cilastatin, IPM/CS) into these aberrant vessels, TAME induces regression of the co-migrating nociceptive nerve fibers, thereby breaking the self-sustaining inflammatory cycle at its vascular root.

Indications

  • Chronic musculoskeletal pain refractory to conservative management (medications, physiotherapy, local injections) for ≥ 3–6 months
  • Large-joint / deep-tissue (standard TAME): knee osteoarthritis, adhesive capsulitis (frozen shoulder), lateral/medial epicondylitis, patellar tendinitis (jumper's knee), chronic low back pain (facet/SI joint origin), trapezius myalgia, chronic pelvic pain syndrome, phantom limb pain
  • Superficial / distal (sTAME): finger/thumb osteoarthritis, De Quervain's tenosynovitis, plantar fasciitis, hallux valgus pain, MTP joint pain

Contraindications

  • Kellgren–Lawrence grade 4 knee OA (prosthetic replacement preferred)
  • Full-thickness tendon or meniscal tear requiring surgical repair
  • Active local infection, severe atherosclerosis, malignancy

Pre-procedure Assessment

MRI is recommended to: (1) exclude surgically correctable structural damage; (2) identify subchondral bone marrow lesions (BMLs) — BML-positive patients show superior pain relief and radiological regression of lesions post-TAME.

Procedure Summary

sTAME (simplified)TAME (standard)
GuidanceUltrasound (outpatient)DSA / fluoroscopy (angiography suite)
Target vesselsDistal radial, ulnar, posterior tibial branchesGenicular, thoracoacromial, subscapular, lumbar arteries, etc.
AnesthesiaLocalLocal ± conscious sedation
RadiationNoneFluoroscopic guidance required

Intraoperative marker: Provocation pain — transient burning/aching at the target site upon injection confirms accurate targeting of the pathological vessel; resolves within minutes.

Clinical Outcomes

ConditionSuccess Rate (6 mo)Durability
Knee osteoarthritis86.3%79.8% at 3 years
Adhesive capsulitis (frozen shoulder)84% (3 mo)88–94% pain-free at 1–3 years
Lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow)88%Tendon structural repair on MRI at 2 years
Chronic low back pain79% (3 mo)57% at 24 months
Thumb base OA100%Stable at 24 months

Post-procedure Care

  • Standard TAME: apply firm compression to arterial access site (groin or wrist) post-procedure; minor ecchymosis typically resolves within 1 week; monitor for pseudoaneurysm if significant hematoma develops
  • Routine heparin administered during standard TAME; rare hypersensitivity to heparin or IPM/CS (rash, pruritus)
  • Analgesic response follows a biphasic pattern: rapid relief within 1 week (neural blockade phase) → sustained improvement over 1–6 months (tissue repair phase); overall 6-month to 2-year success rate 70–90%

entries.title: 肌肉骨骼疾病的物理治療實證指引

S: 旋轉肌袖肌腱病變（肩痛）：肩部功能障礙、手舉困難，適合接受漸進式運動治療
退化性膝關節炎：膝痛、行走及上下樓梯困難，保守治療以運動為核心
各部位肌腱病變：局部肌腱疼痛、活動受限，適合重負荷運動治療
慢性下背痛：持續下背痛超過 3 個月、功能障礙，需規律運動治療
O: 旋轉肌袖：手法治療加運動短期效果優於單獨運動；離心訓練初步試驗顯示效益
膝關節退化：TENS 在 sham 對照試驗中無額外效益，存在安慰劑成分
肌腱病變：治療性超音波系統性回顧顯示效益微小或與安慰劑相比無差異
體外震波治療（ESWT）：運動治療失敗後可作為次要選擇（實證仍混合）
慢性下背痛：多種運動類型效果相近，依患者可及性與偏好選擇
A: 運動治療（主動治療）是所有肌肉骨骼疾病的核心，優先於被動電療儀器
電氣物理治療儀器（TENS、超音波、短波透熱）缺乏高品質隨機試驗一致性效益
被動療法（脊椎推拿、針灸、按摩）可短期輔助，應作為橋接主動治療的工具
心理社會因素顯著或功能障礙明顯時，認知行為治療是有效的輔助治療
P: 漸進式重負荷阻力訓練（向心+離心），依患者耐受度逐步增加負荷
膝關節退化：低衝擊有氧運動（游泳、腳踏車）配合下肢肌力訓練
慢性下背痛：規律運動治療，任何類型皆可，依個人喜好選擇
輔助療法：淺層熱療可短期緩解症狀，特別是急性發作期
不建議常規使用：TENS、治療性超音波、短波透熱療法

電氣物理治療儀器（TENS、超音波、短波透熱）缺乏高品質隨機試驗一致性效益
漸進式重負荷阻力訓練（向心+離心），依患者耐受度逐步增加負荷

versions.simple_zh:
🏃 肌肉骨骼疼痛的物理治療：實證怎麼說？

不論是肩膀旋轉肌袖肌腱炎、退化性膝關節炎、各種肌腱病變，或是慢性下背痛，運動治療才是真正有效的核心療法——而非那些電療、超音波機器。

📌 我適合這份指引嗎？

  • 旋轉肌袖肌腱炎（肩痛、手舉困難）
  • 退化性膝關節炎（膝痛、行走困難）
  • 各部位肌腱炎（肌腱疼痛、活動受限）
  • 慢性下背痛（持續腰痛超過 3 個月）

🔑 最重要的觀念：主動運動 > 被動電療

許多人以為電療、超音波、短波治療機器效果很好，但根據大型研究：

  • ❌ TENS（電刺激）：在退化性膝關節炎、慢性下背痛中，效果不優於假治療
  • ❌ 治療性超音波：對肌腱病變缺乏有說服力的證據
  • ❌ 短波透熱療法：隨機試驗顯示無額外效益

✅ 實際有效的治療

  • 旋轉肌袖：漸進式重量訓練（含肩胛骨穩定運動）+ 手法治療
  • 膝關節退化：低衝擊有氧（游泳、腳踏車）+ 下肢肌力訓練
  • 肌腱病變：漸進式重負荷阻力訓練（含離心運動）
  • 慢性下背痛：規律運動（任何類型皆可，依個人喜好選擇）

🌡️ 輔助療法（可作為配角）

  • 淺層熱療：短期緩解症狀，適合運動前使用或發作期
  • 脊椎推拿、針灸、按摩：可短期改善症狀，但應以橋接主動運動為目標

⚠️ 特殊情況：運動治療無效時

  • 肌腱病變：可考慮體外震波治療（ESWT）作為次要選擇（證據仍混合）
  • 心理社會因素明顯（如焦慮、憂鬱影響疼痛）：建議加入認知行為治療（CBT）
versions.professional_zh:
肌肉骨骼疾病的物理治療實證指引

本指引整合旋轉肌袖肌腱病變、膝關節退化性關節炎、肌腱病變及慢性下背痛等常見肌肉骨骼疾病的物理治療循證建議，重點在於區分有充分實證的主動治療與缺乏實證的被動治療。

一、旋轉肌袖肌腱病變

核心治療：以漸進式、個別化的運動治療為主，包含：

  • 旋轉肌袖肌力訓練 + 肩胛骨穩定訓練（注意動力鏈協調缺損）
  • 活動度訓練（以恢復全關節活動度為目標，先於肌力訓練）
  • 離心訓練可加入（初步試驗顯示效益，仍需進一步研究）
  • 手法治療可輔助減輕疼痛；手法治療 + 運動短期效果優於單獨運動

不建議常規使用：電刺激（phonophoresis、iontophoresis）、治療性超音波（缺乏有說服力的實證）

有限/條件性實證的輔助：高熱療法（微波加熱）——在棘上肌肌腱炎有試驗數據顯示短期優於超音波或運動，但尚需進一步研究

二、膝關節退化性關節炎

核心治療：持續運動治療，無單一最佳運動處方

  • 低衝擊有氧訓練（游泳、腳踏車）+ 下肢肌力訓練
  • 太極拳和瑜伽可作為輔助運動選項
  • 轉介物理治療師以個別化運動計畫並針對損傷調整

不建議：TENS（sham 對照試驗顯示無額外效益，存在安慰劑成分）、短波透熱療法（隨機試驗中無額外效益）

輔助症狀管理：局部熱療有短期止痛效果；部分患者急性發作或腫脹期可短暫使用冰療

三、肌腱病變

核心治療：運動治療，重點在漸進式重負荷

  • 漸進式重負荷阻力訓練（離心 + 向心等張訓練；等長收縮可納入但不確定是否更優）
  • 伸展、關節鬆動術及適當的支持可作為輔助
  • 急性期後，冰療/熱療不是重要治療，但可作為症狀緩解的輔助

次要選擇（運動治療失敗時）：體外震波治療（ESWT）可考慮，但實證仍混合

不支持常規使用：治療性超音波（缺乏決定性實證，系統性回顧顯示效益微小或與安慰劑相比無差異）

四、慢性下背痛

核心治療：規律運動治療，大多數運動類型效果相近，依患者可及性與偏好選擇

  • 輔助短期症狀緩解：淺層熱療（含發作期）
  • 被動療法（脊椎推拿、針灸、按摩）：可考慮用於短期症狀改善，理想上作為主動治療的橋接
  • 心理社會風險或明顯功能障礙：加入認知行為治療（CBT）

不建議常規使用：TENS、治療性超音波、短波透熱療法（隨機試驗缺乏一致性效益）

五、物理治療儀器實證摘要

儀器實證強度臨床立場
微波透熱療法不足（慢性疼痛）不建議常規用於慢性疼痛
短波透熱療法隨機試驗無一致性效益慢性下背痛、膝關節退化均不建議
治療性超音波不足（肌腱病變/慢性疼痛）不建議常規使用
TENS不確定（大多數慢性疼痛）膝關節退化及慢性下背痛不建議；原發性痛經有效
淺層熱療短期症狀緩解可作為輔助，特別是發作期
versions.english:
Evidence-Based Physical Therapy for Musculoskeletal Conditions

This guide summarizes evidence-based physical therapy recommendations for rotator cuff tendinopathy, knee osteoarthritis, tendinopathy, and chronic low back pain, distinguishing active treatments with robust evidence from passive modalities lacking consistent support.

1. Rotator Cuff Tendinopathy

Core treatment: Progressive, individualized exercise therapy including:

  • Rotator cuff strengthening + scapular stabilizer training (address coordination and kinetic chain deficits)
  • Mobility work to restore full range of motion before strengthening
  • Eccentric exercise may be included (preliminary trial evidence; further study needed)
  • Manual therapy as adjunct; manual therapy + exercise modestly superior to exercise alone short-term

Not recommended for routine care: Electrophysical adjuncts (electrical stimulation, phonophoresis, iontophoresis); therapeutic ultrasound (no convincing evidence)

Adjuncts with limited/conditional evidence: Microwave-based hyperthermia — trial data suggest short-term benefit vs. ultrasound or exercise in supraspinatus tendinopathy; further research needed before routine use

2. Knee Osteoarthritis

Core treatment: Ongoing exercise; no single best exercise prescription

  • Low-impact aerobic training + lower-limb strengthening
  • Tai chi and yoga as complementary exercise options
  • Physical therapy referral to personalize program to patient impairments and preferences

Not recommended: TENS (sham-controlled trials show no added benefit; placebo component evident); shortwave diathermy (no added benefit vs. sham)

Adjunct symptom management: Local heat for short-term pain benefit; icing temporarily helpful during flares or swelling

3. Tendinopathy

Core treatment: Exercise therapy with progressive heavy loading

  • Progressive heavy-load resistance exercise (eccentric and/or concentric isotonic; isometrics may be included but not clearly superior)
  • Supportive interventions: stretching, joint mobilization, appropriate orthoses
  • Ice/heat not important after the acute phase; may be used as adjuncts for symptom relief

Secondary option (when exercise fails): Extracorporeal shock wave therapy (ESWT) — mixed evidence

Not routinely supported: Therapeutic ultrasound (systematic reviews: marginal or no benefit vs. placebo)

4. Chronic Low Back Pain

Core treatment: Regular exercise program; most exercise types similarly effective — selection guided by availability and patient preference

  • Adjuncts for short-term symptom relief: superficial heat (including during flares)
  • Passive therapies (spinal manipulation, acupuncture, massage): may be considered for short-term symptomatic improvement, ideally as a bridge to active therapies
  • When psychosocial risk or significant functional impairment: add cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT)

Not routinely recommended: TENS, therapeutic ultrasound, shortwave diathermy (randomized trials show no consistent benefit)

5. PT Modality Evidence Summary

ModalityEvidence SignalClinical Position
Microwave diathermyInsufficient for chronic painNot recommended as routine chronic pain treatment
Shortwave diathermyLittle consistent benefit (RCTs)Not recommended for chronic LBP or knee OA
Therapeutic ultrasoundInsufficient (tendinopathy/chronic pain)Not recommended for routine use
TENSUncertain for most chronic painNot recommended for knee OA or chronic LBP; beneficial for primary dysmenorrhea
Superficial heatShort-term symptom reliefUseful adjunct, especially during flares

hematoma stimulating pain-sensitive receptors in the vessel wall.[6] Headache occurs in approximately **65% of patients** with cervical artery dissection and typically precedes cerebrovascular ischemic symptoms.[7] The pathophysiology involves an intimal tear initiating an intramural hematoma that expands, distending the artery and activating perivascular nociceptors.[6] The headache is likely mediated by **release of pro-inflammatory neurotransmitters from nerve terminals near the injured vessel**.[8] For internal carotid dissections, pain is characteristically **facial, frontal, or temporal**, while vertebral artery dissections cause **cervical or occipital pain**.[7] The headache is typically **moderate to severe** (70.6% for anterior circulation, 88.4% for posterior circulation), with acute onset (98.3%), and can be throbbing or pulsating in nature (23.5-32.6%).[9] Pain is often **pulling in quality** (49.1%) and extends to the neck area (66.5%).[10] Importantly, pathogenic mechanisms** involving aging, genetics, and dysregulated innate and adaptive immunity against unknown triggers.[12][13] The diseases are part of a **disease spectrum**, with 40-60% of GCA patients having PMR and 16-21% of PMR patients having GCA.[13] **Genetic factors** include susceptibility to ***HLA-DRB1*04 and HLA-DRB1*01 alleles**, though this association is stronger in GCA than PMR.*[12][13] The pathogenesis involves: - **Immunosenescence**: Increased autoreactive cells in aging immune systems[12] - **Innate immunity**: Altered toll-like receptor expression, neutrophil [1][3] However, the **absence of a recent infection or vaccination** is atypical, as 76% of GBS patients report an antecedent illness within 4 weeks.
Prominent proprioceptive disturbances and sensory ataxia favor acute-onset CIDP over GBS.

A well-defined sensory level would distinguish myelopathy from peripheral neuropathy.

versions.professional_zh: Polyneuropathy https://www.openevidence.com/ask/7aa2fb1f-8a0c-441f-be67-0e15561fc381 Prominent proprioceptive disturbances and sensory ataxia favor acute-onset CIDP over GBS. This distinction has major therapeutic implications, as CIDP requires corticosteroids while classic GBS does not. Rituximab evidence supportive but selective/contested — subsequent meta-analysis supported benefit, especially in anti-IgG4 antibody-positive disease, whereas a 2025 randomized trial in unselected CIDP found no advantage over placebo after immunoglobulin withdrawal This presentation is concerning for **Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS)**, though several important alternative diagnoses must be excluded given the atypical features. **Most Likely Diagnosis** **Guillain-Barré syndrome** is the leading consideration. The progressive bilateral symmetric numbness with decreased deep tendon reflexes over 1 week fits the classic pattern of acute inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy (AIDP).[1][2] The initial lumbago and lower limb symptoms with ascending progression to upper limbs is characteristic.[1][3] However, the **absence of a recent infection or vaccination** is atypical, as 76% of GBS patients report an antecedent illness within 4 weeks.[2] The preserved urinary function argues against cauda equina syndrome.[1][4] **Other Important Considerations** - **Acute-onset CIDP (chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy)**: Up to 18% of CIDP cases present acutely, mimicking GBS in the first 4-8 weeks. Prominent proprioceptive disturbances and sensory ataxia favor acute-onset CIDP over GBS. This distinction has major therapeutic implications, as CIDP requires corticosteroids while classic GBS does not.[5][6][7] - **Spinal cord compression or cauda equina syndrome**: Must be excluded urgently. The lumbago and bilateral lower limb symptoms could represent compressive pathology. However, the absence of urinary retention, preserved sphincter function, and bilateral upper limb involvement make this less likely.[1][4][8] - **Acute transverse myelitis**: Typically presents with hyperreflexia and Babinski signs (indicating central pathology) rather than decreased reflexes. A well-defined sensory level would distinguish myelopathy from peripheral neuropathy.[9] - **Metabolic/toxic neuropathy**: Diabetes, B12 deficiency, or toxins can cause acute neuropathy but typically progress more slowly. Hypokalemia can mimic GBS and is often overlooked.[10][11][1] - **Acute sensory neuronopathy**: Would present with more prominent sensory ataxia and patchy symptoms, typically without the symmetric ascending pattern.[12] **Next Steps** Assuming this is an acute presentation requiring urgent evaluation, and that basic vital signs and neurologic examination have been completed: **Urgent imaging and consultation:** - **MRI spine with and without contrast** (lumbar, thoracic, cervical) to exclude compressive lesions, spinal cord pathology, and evaluate for nerve root enhancement. Cauda equina and nerve root enhancement supports GBS but is not specific.[13][14][2] - **Neurology consultation** for comprehensive assessment and treatment planning[14] **Initial workup:** - **Lumbar puncture**: CSF typically shows albuminocytologic dissociation (elevated protein, normal cell count) in GBS, though protein may be normal in the first week. CSF helps exclude infectious/malignant causes.[1][2][15] - **Nerve conduction studies/EMG**: Essential for confirming peripheral neuropathy and distinguishing demyelinating from axonal patterns. Early studies may be normal or show only absent F-waves and H-reflexes.[1][2][16] - **Laboratory tests**: Complete blood count, comprehensive metabolic panel (including glucose, renal/hepatic function, electrolytes especially potassium), thyroid function, vitamin B12 with methylmalonic acid, serum protein electrophoresis with immunofixation[10][11][17] - **Pulmonary function tests** (negative inspiratory force, vital capacity) to assess respiratory muscle involvement[14] **Red flags requiring escalation:** - Rapidly progressive weakness, respiratory muscle involvement, autonomic dysfunction, or bulbar symptoms warrant ICU-level monitoring[14][15] - New urinary retention or saddle anesthesia suggests cauda equina requiring urgent surgical evaluation[18][19][8] Do you have information about the patient's strength examination, cranial nerve function, or any autonomic symptoms (blood pressure changes, heart rate variability)? Also, has there been any recent travel, medication changes, or family history of neuropathy? ### References 1. Guillain–Barré Syndrome. Yuki N, Hartung HP. S: Your question about whether **ECA collateral angiogenesis can contribute to headache** is clinically relevant.
- **Chronic post-stroke central neuropathic pain syndrome** - Can develop months after stroke[6][7]
- **Tension-type headache** - Most common headache pattern after ischemic stroke is mild-to-moderate bilateral pain, though unilateral presentations occur[6]
- **Cervicogenic headache** - Related to cervical spondylosis or posture changes after stroke
- **Recurrent ischemic stroke or TIA** - Progression of near-occlusion or new thromboembolic event[8][9]
- **Carotid artery dissection** - Can present with ipsilateral headache and neck pain[10][11]
- **Intracranial aneurysm** - Particularly in collateral pathways under increased hemodynamic stress[12]
Assuming no new focal neurological deficits are present on current presentation, but recognizing that headache alone can herald recurrent ischemia.

- Characterize headache: onset (sudden vs gradual), quality, severity, temporal pattern, associated symptoms (visual changes, jaw claudication, scalp tenderness, constitutional symptoms like fever/weight loss/fatigue)
- Any new neurological symptoms, even transient (weakness, sensory changes, visual disturbances, language changes)
- Palpate temporal arteries bilaterally for tenderness, nodularity, or decreased pulse
- **Complete blood count with platelets** - Platelet count >400×10³/μL supports GCA[13]
- **Emergent imaging** if any new neurological symptoms develop

A: ## Differential Diagnosis
For this 70-year-old man with left ICA territory stroke and near-occlusion one year ago who now presents with new left-sided headache, the differential includes:
**Most Likely Diagnoses:**
- **Giant cell arteritis (temporal arteritis)** - Age >70, new unilateral headache, and potential for stroke as a complication make this a critical consideration[4][5]
**Other Considerations:**
- **Collateral-related headache** - Theoretical mechanism involving increased flow through ECA-ICA collaterals, though not well-established in literature
- **Late-onset migraine or migraine-like headache** - Stroke can trigger migraine syndrome[7]
**Assumptions:** The patient likely has had basic stroke workup previously (brain imaging, vascular imaging of neck vessels).
- **ESR and CRP** - Markedly elevated (ESR >60-100 mm/hr) strongly suggests GCA; normal values make GCA less likely[13][5]
- **Urgent neurology/stroke consultation** if any concern for recurrent ischemia or new vascular pathology

versions.professional_zh: ## Differential Diagnosis https://www.openevidence.com/ask/358e9780-432d-4dfd-97e9-86fb28920eed Your question about whether **ECA collateral angiogenesis can contribute to headache** is clinically relevant. While direct evidence linking ECA-to-ICA collateral flow to headache is limited, the literature suggests that **headache in the setting of chronic ICA occlusion is less common when atherosclerosis is present** (which reduces vessel wall elasticity).[1] In patients with ICA occlusion, the ECA can supply significant cerebral perfusion through retrograde flow and collateral pathways, but this phenomenon has not been definitively established as a cause of new headache one year post-stroke.[2][3] For this 70-year-old man with left ICA territory stroke and near-occlusion one year ago who now presents with new left-sided headache, the differential includes: **Most Likely Diagnoses:** - **Giant cell arteritis (temporal arteritis)** - Age >70, new unilateral headache, and potential for stroke as a complication make this a critical consideration[4][5] - **Chronic post-stroke central neuropathic pain syndrome** - Can develop months after stroke[6][7] - **Tension-type headache** - Most common headache pattern after ischemic stroke is mild-to-moderate bilateral pain, though unilateral presentations occur[6] - **Cervicogenic headache** - Related to cervical spondylosis or posture changes after stroke - **Medication-related headache** - Particularly if on antiplatelet agents like dipyridamole[7] **Cannot-Miss Diagnoses:** - **Recurrent ischemic stroke or TIA** - Progression of near-occlusion or new thromboembolic event[8][9] - **Carotid artery dissection** - Can present with ipsilateral headache and neck pain[10][11] - **Intracranial hemorrhage** - Including hemorrhagic transformation - **Intracranial aneurysm** - Particularly in collateral pathways under increased hemodynamic stress[12] **Other Considerations:** - **Collateral-related headache** - Theoretical mechanism involving increased flow through ECA-ICA collaterals, though not well-established in literature - **Late-onset migraine or migraine-like headache** - Stroke can trigger migraine syndrome[7] ## Next Steps **Assumptions:** The patient likely has had basic stroke workup previously (brain imaging, vascular imaging of neck vessels). Assuming no new focal neurological deficits are present on current presentation, but recognizing that headache alone can herald recurrent ischemia. **Targeted History:** - Characterize headache: onset (sudden vs gradual), quality, severity, temporal pattern, associated symptoms (visual changes, jaw claudication, scalp tenderness, constitutional symptoms like fever/weight loss/fatigue) - Any new neurological symptoms, even transient (weakness, sensory changes, visual disturbances, language changes) - Medication review, particularly recent additions or changes **Focused Exam:** - Palpate temporal arteries bilaterally for tenderness, nodularity, or decreased pulse - Assess for scalp tenderness - Complete neurological examination to detect any new deficits - Fundoscopic examination if GCA suspected **Initial Workup:** - **ESR and CRP** - Markedly elevated (ESR >60-100 mm/hr) strongly suggests GCA; normal values make GCA less likely[13][5] - **Complete blood count with platelets** - Platelet count >400×10³/μL supports GCA[13] - **Brain MRI with DWI** - Most sensitive for acute/subacute ischemia[9] - **CTA head and neck or MRA** - Assess for progression of stenosis, new dissection, aneurysm formation, or collateral pathway changes[10][12][14][15][16] - **Temporal artery ultrasound** (if available) - "Halo sign" has high specificity for GCA[17][5] **Red Flags/Escalation:** - **Immediate temporal artery biopsy** if GCA suspected based on clinical features and elevated inflammatory markers - do not delay treatment while awaiting biopsy[4][5] - **Urgent neurology/stroke consultation** if any concern for recurrent ischemia or new vascular pathology - **Emergent imaging** if any new neurological symptoms develop Figure 1 Clinical Disease Characteristics and Suggested Algorithm for Diagnostic Evaluation of Patients With Symptoms of Polymyalgia Rheumatica or Suspected Giant Cell Arteritis undefined The diagnostic algorithm above illustrates the systematic approach to evaluating suspected GCA, which should be a priority given the patient's age and presentation. Do you have information about the character of the headache (throbbing vs pressure-like), any visual symptoms, temporal artery tenderness on exam, or whether inflammatory markers have been checked? ### References 1. Is there anything to add or change?
Proceed with urgent neuroimaging and parallel neurologic assessment rather than symptomatic treatment alone.
First-line imaging and SAH rule-out pathway

Assess for features associated with higher odds of subarachnoid hemorrhage (eg, impaired consciousness, neck stiffness, nausea/vomiting, exertional onset, elevated blood pressure, occipital location).
O: Obtain noncontrast head CT immediately to evaluate for subarachnoid hemorrhage and other causes (including ischemic stroke).
If CT is negative, lumbar puncture is recommended in most cases to evaluate CSF for subarachnoid hemorrhage, particularly when CT is performed more than 6 hours after onset.
Lumbar puncture may be omitted only in select patients when a technically adequate CT is obtained within 6 hours of onset and read as negative by an experienced radiologist, with appropriate caveats.
If the cause remains uncertain after initial SAH evaluation, obtain additional brain and neurovascular imaging, with brain MRI favored when available.
Include MR venography or CT venography to evaluate for cerebral venous sinus thrombosis in patients presenting with thunderclap headache.

Given dizziness and gait ataxia, maintain high suspicion for posterior circulation ischemic stroke and pursue neuroimaging accordingly; MRI is often needed when CT is unrevealing and suspicion remains high.
Focused bedside assessment elements that change pretest probability
A clearly abnormal neurologic examination or severely ataxic gait supports a central process and warrants neuroimaging and neurologic evaluation.
A: This presentation should be treated as a neurologic emergency because thunderclap headache requires immediate evaluation for subarachnoid hemorrhage and other secondary causes, and acute gait ataxia raises concern for posterior circulation stroke or cerebellar hemorrhage.
Neurovascular imaging for alternative causes and posterior circulation process
